narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 53.4% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection for many applicants. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in thousands of past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which places you at the lower end of your school's rankings, far below the typical 6th decile among those who passed the bar. This stood out as the strongest signal to the model, suggesting challenges in sustaining performance later in your studies. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 was a notable strength, above the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it significantly pulled the prediction toward success. Your upper-middle family income also helped, aligning well with the majority of successful students and nudging the odds in your favor compared to lower brackets. Being full-time aligns with over 90% of those who passed, so it didn't hurt, while your LSAT score of 26 fell short of the 37 average for passers, though it wasn't among the top drivers here. Overall, your low Year 3 decile outweighed these positives, mirroring profiles of students who struggled on the bar despite solid earlier GPAs. Compared to typical accepted applicants, who often show stronger mid-to-late grade deciles alongside high LSATs, your trajectory raised more red flags. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward 37 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted courses or tutoring, and reapplying with updated transcripts that demonstrate upward momentum. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so focus on building that evidence of bar readiness. Your above-average undergrad performance shows you have the foundation—now channel it into the areas the model flagged most. With persistence, you can shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
